front: Play: Theophile Gautier called its only 'tolerable' line 'the daughter of Minos and Pasiphae,' describing the title character; considered untranslatable
Play: a chariot is attacked by a horned sea monster (half-bull, half-snake); Theramene describes the scene offstage as horses drag the victim to his death
Play: the nurse Oenone convinces the title character to declare her love for her stepson, then hatches a plot to accuse him of rape; Oenone later drowns herself
Play: introduces the character Aricie as Hippolytus's love interest, a notable difference from its source, Euripides' Hippolytus
Play: Theseus invokes Neptune's curse against his own son Hippolytus
Play: Ted Hughes spent his last months translating this play's rhymed alexandrine verse
Play: Hermione asks Orestes to kill Pyrrhus, who loves the title character; after Pyrrhus dies, Hermione kills herself and Orestes goes insane
Play: Hermione tells Cleone she 'loved Pyrrhus too much not to hate him'
Play: the title role was written for actress Therese du Parc, the playwright's lover, whom he stole from Moliere's company
Play: features nobly-born servants Cleone and Cephisa; the playwright returned to writing 'suivante' roles
Play: Voltaire called it 'the supreme achievement of the human mind'; the high priest Joad installs Joash as king after dethroning the title queen who worships Baal
Play: the title character describes her mother wearing makeup to 'repair the irreparable ravages of time'
Play: Nero poisons the title character at a feast and abducts his fiancee Junia, who becomes a Vestal Virgin; Narcisse is killed by a mob
Play: this playwright's only comedy, adapted from Aristophanes' The Wasps; a judge rules against the dog Citron for eating a chicken but pardons it after pleas from its puppies
Play: features the mad Judge Dandin and the porter Petit Jean; Leander and Isabelle trick Chicaneau into allowing their marriage
Play: unconventional three-act form; the title character convinces King Ahasuerus to stop a plotted massacre of Jews; written for Madame de Maintenon's school
Play: the Roman Emperor Titus is forced to renounce the Queen of Judaea because 'Rome took a hatred to the name of King'
Author: Roland Barthes' psychoanalysis-inflected study of this playwright was attacked by Raymond Picard in 'New Criticism or New Fraud?'; the controversy helped legitimize literary theory
Author: Lucien Goldmann's The Hidden God compares the decline of this playwright's social class to how his characters reject the world they are compelled to live in
Author: in Proust's In Search of Lost Time, the narrator befriends Berma, an actress specializing in this playwright's plays; schoolgirls write letters from Sophocles to this playwright
Author: converted to Jansenism late in life and spent his final years writing the Spiritual Canticles
Author: Alan Hollinghurst's best-known translations are of this playwright's plays; Cambridge Five spy John Cairncross also translated them for Penguin Classics
Author: known for small vocabulary and simple diction, depicting characters 'trapped' by passion; closely adhered to the dramatic unities
